headline: GRAINS DRIFT LOWER, GAS SPIKES ON CHINA FUEL CAPS
subheadline: Corn drops a nickel while natural gas explodes 4.6% as Beijing raises fuel price ceilings again.
lead: Corn closed $4.63, down a nickel, as fund liquidation continued without weather premium to support it. Natural gas spiked 4.6% to $3.17 after China raised fuel price caps for the second time this month, signaling Beijing's struggle with war-driven energy costs. The grain weakness looks like position-clearing ahead of tomorrow's crop progress report, not new fundamental pressure.
the_takeaway: Gas tells the energy story corn won't: war premium is structural, not seasonal.
teaser: Funds liquidate grain positions while energy markets price permanent war risk.

one_number.unit: annual U.S. ag export losses from China tariffs
one_number.context: North Dakota State's new study shows China's retaliatory tariffs are hitting U.S. agriculture harder than the previous trade war. The $15 billion annual loss explains why Monday's $17 billion Chinese commitment couldn't hold grain rallies past Tuesday. The math is simple: promises don't offset active tariff damage.

yesterdays_call.summary: Called Hormuz breakthrough removing supply shock premium from energy markets.
yesterdays_call.note: China's fuel cap hikes show the supply shock is getting worse, not better.

sections.title: Grains Fade on Fund Flow
sections.body: Corn closed $4.63, essentially flat, as managed money kept rotating out of grain positions. Soybeans fell 3 cents to $11.94, wheat dropped 2¾ cents to $6.52. **No weather catalyst, no export news, just fund liquidation** ahead of tomorrow's crop progress print. The Missouri Bootheel is replanting due to dry conditions, but that's local noise against a national planting pace that's still on schedule. December corn held $4.86, suggesting the carry structure isn't broken yet.
sections.bottom_line: Position-clearing, not fundamental weakness driving the drift.

sections.title: Energy Splits on China Demand
sections.body: Natural gas exploded 4.6% to $3.17 after China raised fuel price caps again, the second hike this month as Beijing struggles with war-driven energy costs. Crude barely moved at $99.26, up a dime, but the **gas spike tells the real story**: China's importing fuel oil to cover natural gas shortfalls as domestic production reverses direction. Japan's Middle East crude imports hit record lows in April as the Strait of Hormuz chokes supply. The energy war premium isn't fading, it's getting structural.
sections.catalyst: China raises fuel price caps for second time this month amid supply crisis
sections.bottom_line: Gas pricing permanent war risk while crude stays complacent.

sections.title: Livestock Holds Amid Grain Weakness
sections.body: Live cattle dropped 80 basis points to $245.18 but held above the $245 floor that's been the line since Monday. Feeders gained half a percent to $365.82, **moving opposite to corn's weakness** as expected with the inverse relationship intact. Class III milk jumped 1.6% to $16.92, the strongest move in the livestock complex. Cargill initiated a lockout at the Fort Morgan beef plant, but that's a labor issue, not a demand signal. The cattle trade is waiting for direct business to reset the cash market.
sections.catalyst: Feeder strength on grain weakness maintains inverse relationship pattern
sections.bottom_line: Cattle acting patient, not panicked, while feeders lead on grain weakness.

outside_the_pit.title: China Tariffs Hit U.S. Ag Harder Than Previous Trade War
outside_the_pit.body: North Dakota State's new study quantifies $15 billion in annual losses from Chinese retaliatory tariffs, worse than the original trade war damage. This explains why Monday's $17 billion commitment couldn't sustain grain rallies past Tuesday.

outside_the_pit.title: Midwest Dust Storms Raise Topsoil Loss Concerns
outside_the_pit.body: Central Illinois farmers report the worst spring dust storm activity they've seen, with repeated events across the grain belt. The soil loss implications won't show up in yield monitors until harvest, but the erosion is happening now.

outside_the_pit.title: Farm Program Uncertainty Leaves Producers Guessing on ARC, PLC
outside_the_pit.body: Delayed farm bill and slow program detail releases have producers unable to make informed ARC versus PLC elections. The policy vacuum is keeping planting decisions conservative when flexibility matters most.

spread_to_watch.label: December corn / July corn carry
spread_to_watch.commentary: The carry held at 23 cents even as nearby corn sold off, suggesting the fund liquidation is hitting front months harder than deferred. When the funds finish rotating out, this spread should narrow back toward 18-20 cents normal.

basis.headline: Eastern Belt firms while Western stays soft on ethanol demand
basis.body: **Eastern Belt corn basis is tightening** as ethanol plants come back online after seasonal maintenance. Western Belt staying soft, consistent with the seasonal pattern. Producers east of the Mississippi with old-crop bushels have a window the futures board alone isn't pricing.

weekly_thread.question: Will China's $17 billion ag commitment shift fund positioning back into grains this week?
weekly_thread.status_text: The $15 billion annual tariff damage study published today explains why China's $17 billion commitment failed to hold. Promises don't offset active trade war losses.

the_more_you_know.title: The 23-cent spread that's calling the fund rotation
the_more_you_know.body: December corn closed 23 cents over July today, the same level it's held for three sessions despite nearby weakness. When funds liquidate grain positions, they typically hit front months harder than deferred contracts, widening the carry structure. But this spread is holding firm, suggesting the liquidation pressure is more measured than panic-driven. Watch for the carry to narrow back toward 18-20 cents once the position-clearing finishes, that's your signal the fund rotation is complete.

watch_list.desc: USDA Crop Progress: corn above 45% planted keeps weather premium out
Weekly export sales: soy under 400K MT extends the fund liquidation theme
China fuel policy announcements: more price cap hikes mean structural energy crisis

daily_quote.text: Don't put all your eggs in one basket.

source_summary: CME futures, USDA reports, industry news wires, energy market data
